question.text: Scenario: Vikram (age 32) is a software engineer earning Rs. 18,00,000 per annum. His wife Priya (age 30) also works and earns Rs. 8,00,000 per annum. They have one child aged 2 years, who is their only dependent. They own a house valued at Rs. 40,00,000 with an outstanding home loan of Rs. 15,00,000. Their current investments include: Rs. 5,00,000 in bank fixed deposits, Rs. 3,00,000 in equity mutual funds, Rs. 2,00,000 in PPF, and an emergency fund of Rs. 1,50,000. Their financial goals are: child's higher education (16 years away), retirement (28 years away), and a car upgrade (2 years away). Vikram completes a risk profiling questionnaire administered by his investment adviser Suresh, and scores 72 out of 100. Suresh must now analyze this profile to make suitable investment recommendations. Question 2 of 5: Given Vikram's aggressive risk profile, which asset allocation model from the standard model portfolios would be MOST appropriate for his long-term goals (education and retirement)?

answer.text: 15% equity index, 5% gold, 35% debt hybrid/MIS/SCSS, 30% debt/FD, 15% liquid
50% equity, 20% sector, 10% gold ETF, 10% debt, 10% liquid

explanation.text: **The Logic:** For Vikram's aggressive profile with long-term goals (16 and 28 years), the high-risk model portfolio is most appropriate: 50% equity, 20% sector, 10% gold ETF, 10% debt, 10% liquid. This gives 70% equity exposure, suitable for his risk appetite and long time horizons. **The Trap:** Option C (35% equity, 10% sector, 15% gold, 30% debt, 10% liquid) is the moderate-risk model for a young married family — close to Vikram's life stage but not matching his aggressive risk profile. Option D is the conservative model. Option A is for a couple in their 70s. Students must match risk profile to model, not life stage to model. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series X-B tests the ability to match risk profiles to specific model portfolios. Knowing the exact allocations of each model portfolio is essential for the exam. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** While the aggressive model portfolio is appropriate for long-term goals, the car upgrade goal (2 years away) requires a separate, conservative allocation. Don't apply a single model across all goals — segment the portfolio by goal and time horizon.
